narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 56.6% chance of not passing. Picture your story through its eyes: the biggest red flag was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class that year, which screamed louder than anything else that the rigors of advanced legal studies might have been an uphill battle. That was compounded by your LSAT score of 30, which, while respectable in some lights, fell short of the firepower the model associates with bar success, pulling strongly toward this tougher outlook. Your Year 1 decile of 2 didn't help either, showing early struggles that set a pattern of needing to climb from the lower ranks. Even with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5 and a middle-family income bracket—factors that offer some stability—they weren't enough to counterbalance, as they too nudged the prediction downward in this mix. Your background as a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in subtly but similarly, aligning with profiles the model links to higher hurdles. It's this full picture, not any one thing, that painted you as a higher-risk bet for passing the bar on the first try, leading to our tough call on your application. But here's the empowering part: you're not defined by these numbers—you've already shown grit with that 3.5 UGPA, and targeting law full-time shows your fire. Many in similar spots have turned it around by doubling down on study strategies or targeted prep; this isn't a door closed forever, just a signal to build even stronger momentum. What do you think your next step could be?
